text:p.text:span: X1 = A 7475 i 44 â€“ ii 1â€“24 = IbIأ¥1A
The tablet has deteriorated in parts since O. Gurney copied it and since I first collated it many years ago.

office:text.text:p: Tablet typology: compilation tablet (Type I): X1; Type III: X2.

text:p.text:span: : X2 edited by S. N. Kramer,
s[aying (the words of) Ibbi-Sin, your king]:
The enemy, although not â€¦, has mustered (his army), and has [brought disorder] in all the foreign lands.
But then you, according to your statement, had an omen
(telling you that) now Enlil [has returned] to the side of his son Sin.
You (text: he) received twenty talents of silver toآ­ purchase grain.
You (text: he) purchased grain at the price of twoآ­
Alt. transl.: return it to me.
as if (you hadآ­ purchased it) at one
The hostile Amorite, having stationed himself in the frontier territory,
having come to know the strength of my homeland and its troopsâ€”
how could you let them sweep into my frontier zone and capture all my large fortresses?
Why has Puzur-Marduk, general of Badigihursaأ‘a, not confronted the Amorites?
it is because of your self-confidence the monkey has [
Now I am overhauling 180 great barges, each (vessel) with a capacity of 120
, as well as overhauling 600 barges with a capacity of 60
and therefore you should measure out the silver that you have as well
as what (was set aside for even) the most exalted allotments
Do not dare to come back here until you have dispatched (the grain) to me! آ­
Guarding Nippur and Isin is your responsibility. It is urgent!
â€œas long as Enlil is my master, where else (in the world but with you) would I go?â€‌
Right now Enlil is angry with me,آ­
and is handing over Ur to آ­the enemy.
The enemy, although not (yet) within it, has mustered (his army), and created disorder in all the foreign lands.
But you, according to your statement, had an omen
that Enlil has reconciled with his son Sin.
You received twenty talents of silver toآ­ purchase grain.
You purchased grain at the price of twoآ­
but you then returned it to me (as if you hadآ­ purchased it) at one
How could you, together with Puzur-Numuأ¥da, general ofآ­ Bad-Igihursaأ‘a,
allow the enemy Amorites to enter into my homeland? آ­
Until he returns to you to fight (them)
there will be آ­idiots (running around) in the homelandâ€”
why has he not confronted the Amorites?
This letter is attested in two different versions. Both accounts are defective and appear to be variant Old Babylonian concoctions that mix together information from I

Both manuscripts are of unknown origin, even if it is possible that X1 comes from Sippar but they share certain features, most noticeably a shaky command of Sumerian verbal morphology. It would appear that the authors of these texts used the third person agreement prefix /n/ indiscriminately, so that forms with this element often have to be interpreted as first or second person verbs, while forms with /e/ are clearly intended as first or third person. The texts make little sense in the context of the CKU, and we are at a loss to discern if this is a function of scribal ineptitude, or if there are ironies here that escape us.

office:text.text:p: A 4â€™. The idea that an ominous sign presages the victory of Ibbi-Sin is derived from the Puzur-Numuإ،da correspondence but is turned around, as here it is Iإ،bi-Erra, not the king, who has received an omen.

A 6â€™â€“7â€™ = B 12â€“13. These lines are important, because they are the source of the commonly repeated notion that Iإ،bi-Erra blackmailed Ibbi-Sin, asking him to pay him double for grain he had already received funds to buy in the first place. Although the grammar of these lines is characteristically flimsy, there can be no doubt that according to both sources the issue is not blackmail at all; the king of Ur is desperate and offers to take it at double the original price. It is unclear if the writer of A intended the verbal form in line 7â€™ as the participial gi.أ‘u.ed.am, or as an imperative gi.mu.da.

text:p.text:span: ) is used here in the same sense as Akkadian
/, â€œmonkey,â€‌ are partially destroyed and difficult to read. From what I can discern it is either

office:text.text:p: A 15â€™â€“16â€™. As in the previous letter, the numbers in these lines do not add up properly. According to line 20â€™ we are dealing here with 72,000 gur of grain; that is all that Iإ،bi-Erra wants to send back at the rate of two gur per shekel, as he is obviously keeping either half the silver he received or half of the grain that he purchased. 180 boats of 120 gur capacity would carry 21,600 gur. According to line 16â€™ there were 600 (gأ©أ¥-u) vessels, each of 60 gur capacity, and therefore the tonnage would be 36,000 gur. Thus the combined carrying capacity of all the boats would be 57,600 gur. The only way that I can make some sense of this is to assume that the scribe made mistake that involved confusion between the decimal and sexigesimal systems: he wrote 3أ—ؤ‍أ‰أ…, that is 180 but was thinking of 3أ—100 (i.e. 3 <me>) = 300, and thus the total in line 16â€™ would also be 36,000. If this were indeed the case, both lines would add up to 72,000 gur. Alternatively, we are dealing with a scribal error; the correct number would be 5أ—ؤ‍أ‰أ…, that is 300.
A 17â€™â€“19â€™. This passage is clearly derived from PuIb1 (letter 23) 36â€“38. Note that here, as in the source letter, X1 writes Inu-أ؛r-أ©-a for Nur-aأ¦um of Eإ،nunna.

text:p.text:span: must be a calque from Akkadian

functioning as a locative, as is often does in Larsa and late OB Sumerian texts. Unfortunately, the translation â€œthey have handed over to youâ€‌ makes little sense in the context. The present translation assumes that this is a mistake and that the intention here was to use a Sumerian equivalent of the Akkadian use of
in the technical sense of â€œto load a boatâ€‌ (
. The reading and translation of the second half of this line are most uncertain.
. There is some ambiguity in the reading of the verbalآ­ root. There are two possible idioms that could come into playآ­ here,
, â€œwhereto, whither; where,â€‌ and M. Civil,

25 (1974â€“1977) آ­71. For a very different interpretation of lines B 3â€“4 see C. Wilcke, â€œPolitik im Spiegel der Literatur,â€‌ 64: â€œBis dahinâ€”(bei) mein(em) Konig Enlil!â€”wirst du gehen, wirst du derart die Worte verdrehen?â€‌
provides a direct link to the previous letter, from I
bi-Erra to the king, which ends with the line (55):
is formally first or second person but this is most probably a mistake for third person. Alternatively, it is possible that the scribe intended â€œhow could you allow PNâ€¦â€‌
, â€œweakling, poor person,â€‌ in the late lexical entry in SIG
designate an idiot; see, most recently, B. Alster,
here and in the next line

office:text.text:p: Comparison of the Common Part of Versions A and B
